response.description_long:
Data contracts between producers and consumers are the mechanism most teams reach for after a broken dashboard surfaces in a board meeting. Rowfern, founded in Denver in 2022 by former data engineers Owen Park and Talia Brooks, builds tooling around that exact pattern: catch broken schema contracts, silent nulls, and volume anomalies in the pull request, not in the postmortem.

The product reads a dbt project or SQL pipeline and generates a suite of checks — schema contracts (column names, types, nullability, accepted values) between producing and consuming models, freshness and volume expectations learned from history, and custom assertions written in YAML. Checks run in CI on every pull request against a sampled snapshot of production data, completing typically in under five minutes. If a producing model renames a column, the check runs for every downstream consumer; the PR comment lists exactly which contracts broke and who consumes them. The same checks run on schedule in production, paging the owning team through PagerDuty or Slack when a contract breaks. The engine is open source under Apache 2.0; the commercial platform adds a browsable contract catalog, ownership routing, historical baselines, SSO, audit logs, and a read-only in-VPC deployment.

Teams at Chartbeam, Densify Analytics, and Copperline Health run Rowfern on more than 4,000 models. Chartbeam's team gated every dbt PR with contracts within two weeks and reported zero incidents traced to upstream schema changes for three consecutive quarters. Densify runs the engine open-source on two projects and the platform across the rest. Copperline Health, under compliance constraints, runs all checks in-warehouse with the read-only deployment. The website names these customers and cites the Chartbeam outcome explicitly, along with case studies on the blog.

The pricing model separates the open-source engine from the commercial platform. Cloud plans start at $500 per month per workspace, scaling with the number of models under contract (models with at least one enforced check), not check runs or users. Annual plans add SSO, audit logs, and the in-VPC deployment. The engine is free forever for a single team on one project — the typical onboarding funnel is to start with the engine, generate contracts for the most-consumed marts, tighten from warnings to merge gates over a week or two, then expand model by model. The site is explicit that nothing requires a big-bang migration.

Rowfern differentiates from dbt's built-in tests by binding producers to consumers and enforcing boundaries in CI with production-shaped data, plus learned freshness and volume baselines in production. The two coexist: Rowfern imports existing dbt tests into its catalog. The company's taxonomy of silent data failures is shared widely, and the open-source engine has accumulated a meaningful contributor community. The team of 19 is remote across US time zones, and the engine repository includes the contract generator, check runner, and CI integrations — everything a single team needs to enforce contracts on one project.
